title: Ecosystems and Global Biomes
examTip: How/when this appears in AQA Geography Paper 1: ecosystems appear in Topic 2 (The Living World). Expect questions on components of ecosystems, food webs, nutrient cycling, and the distribution of global biomes. 4-mark and 6-mark questions often ask you to explain interdependence or the effects of removing one species.
introTips: Overview: ecosystems are communities of living things interacting with their non-living environment — from a garden pond to the Amazon rainforest.
Skill gained: you will be able to explain how energy flows and nutrients cycle through ecosystems, and map the world's major biomes.
By the end: you can describe what happens when ecosystems are disrupted and explain why biodiversity matters.
intro: Everything on Earth depends on everything else — that's the big idea behind ecosystems. Understanding how energy flows from the sun through producers to consumers, and how nutrients are recycled by decomposers, helps explain why cutting down a forest or polluting a river sends shockwaves through an entire community of organisms. These connections matter for geography because humans both rely on and threaten ecosystems at every scale.

keyPoints.heading: What is an Ecosystem?
keyPoints.content: An ecosystem is a community of living organisms interacting with each other and with their non-living environment. The living parts are called biotic components — these include producers (plants that photosynthesise), consumers (animals that eat producers or other animals), and decomposers (bacteria and fungi that break down dead material). The non-living parts are abiotic components — climate, temperature, rainfall, sunlight, soil type, and water. Energy enters an ecosystem from the sun and flows along a food chain: sun → producer → primary consumer → secondary consumer → top predator. A food web shows the complex, overlapping feeding relationships between many species. Nutrient cycling means decomposers break down dead organisms and return nutrients to the soil, where producers absorb them again — the cycle continues.
writeBullets: Ecosystem — community of living organisms interacting with their non-living environment
Biotic components — living parts: producers, consumers, decomposers
Abiotic components — non-living parts: climate, soil, water, temperature, light
Producer — plant that makes food by photosynthesis; base of every food chain
Consumer — animal that eats producers or other consumers
Decomposer — bacteria/fungi that break down dead matter and return nutrients to soil
Nutrient cycling — continuous recycling of nutrients through organisms and back into soil

keyTerms.def: A community of living organisms and their non-living environment, interacting together.
The living components of an ecosystem (plants, animals, decomposers).
The non-living components of an ecosystem (climate, soil, water, light).
An organism (usually a plant) that makes its own food through photosynthesis.
An organism that obtains energy by eating other organisms.
Organisms such as bacteria and fungi that break down dead material, returning nutrients to the soil.
The movement of nutrients through an ecosystem, from the soil to producers to consumers and back via decomposers.

keyPoints.memTip: Remember biotic = BIOlogical (living), abiotic = A-biotic (not living). For food chains: Sun → Producer → Consumer → Decomposer. Cover your notes and write all 7 bullets from memory.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Examiners love to ask how removing one organism affects the whole food web — always think about knock-on effects both up and down the chain.

keyPoints.heading: Small-Scale UK Ecosystem: Pond or Deciduous Woodland
keyPoints.content: A deciduous woodland is a great small-scale UK ecosystem to know. Producers include oak and ash trees, bluebells, and ferns. Primary consumers (herbivores) include caterpillars, deer, and voles. Secondary consumers eat primary consumers — these include blue tits and foxes. Top predators such as sparrowhawks and tawny owls are at the apex of the food web. All of these species are interdependent — they rely on each other to survive. If one species is removed, there are serious knock-on effects. For example, if caterpillars were wiped out by pesticide, blue tit numbers would fall (less food), oak trees could be damaged less (fewer leaf-eaters), but sparrowhawks would also suffer indirectly. A pond ecosystem works on the same principle: algae → water fleas → small fish → pike. Remove the water fleas and the whole chain shifts.
writeBullets: Deciduous woodland — UK ecosystem with oak/ash trees, caterpillars, blue tits, foxes, sparrowhawks
Primary consumer — herbivore that eats producers (e.g. caterpillar eats oak leaves)
Secondary consumer — eats primary consumers (e.g. blue tit eats caterpillars)
Top predator — at the apex of the food web; nothing eats it (e.g. sparrowhawk)
Interdependence — all species rely on each other; removal of one affects all others
Knock-on effect — a change to one part of the ecosystem ripples through other parts

keyTerms.def: The way in which all living things in an ecosystem depend on each other to survive.
When a change to one part of an ecosystem causes a chain of changes to other parts.
The animal at the top of a food chain that is not hunted by any other animal.
An animal that feeds directly on producers (plants).
An animal that feeds on primary consumers.

keyPoints.memTip: Think of a woodland food web as a spiderweb — if you cut one thread, others sag too. Cover your notes and name the four levels of a UK woodland food chain from memory.
keyPoints.tutor_note: When a question asks 'explain the effects of removing one species', always name the species above AND below the one removed in the food chain — that shows you understand the web, not just a single chain.

keyPoints.heading: Global Ecosystems: Biomes
keyPoints.content: A biome is a large-scale ecosystem with a distinctive climate, soil, vegetation, and wildlife. The distribution of biomes is closely tied to latitude, climate, and distance from the sea. At the equator (0°) you find tropical rainforests — hot (~27°C) and wet (2000mm+ rainfall) all year. Moving to about 30°N/S are the hot deserts — very dry (under 250mm rain), extreme temperatures. The savanna grassland lies between the rainforest and desert zones — a wet season and a dry season. At mid-latitudes (40–60°N) are temperate deciduous forests and temperate grasslands. Further north is the boreal/taiga forest — vast coniferous forests stretching across Canada, Russia, and Scandinavia. Near the poles is the tundra — very cold, permafrost soil, low-growing vegetation. At the poles themselves is polar ice. As you move away from the coast, continental interiors become drier — this is why grasslands and deserts often occur in the heart of continents.
writeBullets: Biome — large-scale ecosystem with distinctive climate, vegetation, and wildlife
Tropical rainforest — equator (0°), hot and wet all year, high biodiversity
Hot desert — ~30°N/S, very dry (under 250mm/year), extreme temperature swings
Savanna grassland — between equator and deserts, wet and dry seasons
Temperate deciduous forest — mid-latitudes (~40–60°N), four seasons, moderate rainfall
Boreal/taiga forest — subarctic latitudes, cold, coniferous trees, short summers
Tundra — polar regions, permafrost, very low vegetation, extreme cold

keyTerms.def: A large-scale ecosystem characterised by a particular climate, vegetation type, and wildlife community.
Distance north or south of the equator, measured in degrees — a key factor controlling biome distribution.
A biome found at the equator with high temperatures and rainfall all year, and extremely high biodiversity.
A biome near the poles with permafrost soil, very low temperatures, and sparse, low-growing vegetation.
A biome of subarctic latitudes dominated by coniferous trees, with short cool summers and very cold winters.

keyPoints.memTip: Picture the globe from equator to pole: Rainforest → Desert → Savanna → Grassland → Deciduous Forest → Taiga → Tundra → Ice. Use the first letters: R-D-S-G-D-T-T-I. Cover your notes and place each biome on a blank world outline from memory.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Questions often ask you to explain WHY biomes are distributed as they are — always link back to latitude, temperature, and rainfall. Don't just name them; explain the climate conditions.

keyPoints.heading: Human Impact on Ecosystems
keyPoints.content: Humans are changing ecosystems at a rate faster than nature can adapt. Deforestation removes trees, destroys habitats, and disrupts nutrient cycles and water cycles. Urbanisation replaces natural habitats with roads and buildings, fragmenting ecosystems and cutting off wildlife corridors. Pollution — from agriculture, industry, and transport — poisons soils and water, killing species at the base of food chains. Overgrazing by livestock removes vegetation, leaving soil exposed to erosion and disrupting plant communities. Climate change shifts temperature and rainfall patterns, pushing biomes out of alignment — species that cannot adapt fast enough face extinction. All of these threats reduce biodiversity — the variety of species in an ecosystem. Biodiversity is important because it makes ecosystems more resilient, provides medicines, food, and clean water, and regulates the climate. Once biodiversity is lost, ecosystems can collapse — with consequences for humans as well as wildlife.
writeBullets: Deforestation — removal of trees; destroys habitat, disrupts nutrient and water cycles
Urbanisation — replacing natural land with buildings; fragments habitats and wildlife corridors
Pollution — chemicals from farming, industry, and transport poison food chains
Overgrazing — livestock remove vegetation; soil erosion follows, plant communities destroyed
Climate change — alters rainfall and temperature, shifting biome boundaries and threatening species
Biodiversity — variety of species; high biodiversity = more resilient ecosystem

keyTerms.def: The clearing of forests, often for farming, logging, or settlement.
The variety of species living in an area — the higher the biodiversity, the more resilient the ecosystem.
The growth of towns and cities, often at the expense of natural habitats.
When too many animals graze an area, removing vegetation faster than it can regrow.
When an ecosystem is so severely disrupted that it can no longer function — species populations crash and may not recover.

keyPoints.memTip: Remember the five human threats with DUPOC: Deforestation, Urbanisation, Pollution, Overgrazing, Climate change. Cover your notes and write each threat with one consequence from memory.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Exam questions on human impact almost always expect you to link the cause to its specific effect on the ecosystem — for example, deforestation disrupts the nutrient cycle because decomposers have less leaf litter to break down.

tables.title: Global Biomes: Climate, Location, and Examples
rows: Wet and dry seasons, warm all year
Cold winters, hot summers, low rainfall
Very cold winters, cool short summers
Extremely cold, permafrost, very low rainfall
Extreme cold, virtually no liquid water

0.question: Which component of an ecosystem breaks down dead organisms and returns nutrients to the soil?
0.correctFeedback: Correct! Decomposers — bacteria and fungi — break down dead material and recycle nutrients back into the soil, completing the nutrient cycle.
0.wrongFeedback: Think about which organisms play the recycling role in an ecosystem. Producers make food, consumers eat it — but decomposers are the ones that break everything back down.

2.question: Which biome is found at approximately 30°N and 30°S of the equator and has less than 250mm of rainfall per year?
2.correctFeedback: Correct! Hot deserts are found at around 30°N/S — a band of high pressure that blocks rainfall. The Sahara and Arabian Desert are classic examples.
2.wrongFeedback: Remember that latitude controls biome distribution. The hot, dry zone at 30°N/S is where descending air creates high pressure and prevents rain — that's desert territory.

3.question: A pesticide kills all the caterpillars in a woodland. What is the most likely knock-on effect higher up the food chain?
options: Blue tit numbers decrease due to food shortage
3.correctFeedback: Exactly right. Blue tits eat caterpillars, so with caterpillars gone, blue tits lose a key food source and their numbers will fall. This is interdependence in action.
3.wrongFeedback: Think about what blue tits eat. If caterpillars are removed from the food web, the animals that rely on them for food will suffer first.

commonMistakes: Confusing biotic and abiotic — remember: biotic = BIOlogical = living things; abiotic = 'not living' (climate, soil, water).
Describing a food chain as going in both directions — energy always flows ONE way: from the sun through producers to consumers. Nutrients cycle, but energy does not.
Naming only one effect when asked about removing a species — always state knock-on effects both up AND down the food web.
Confusing a biome with an ecosystem — a biome is the large global category (e.g. 'tropical rainforest'); an ecosystem is a specific community in a place (e.g. a particular stretch of the Amazon).
Forgetting that climate change is a form of human impact on ecosystems — it belongs alongside deforestation and pollution as a human-caused threat.
examTips: When explaining nutrient cycling, always mention decomposers explicitly — examiners want to see you understand the full loop, not just producers and consumers.
For biome distribution questions, link location to latitude AND climate (temperature + rainfall) — two linked reasons are better than one.
If asked to 'assess' or 'evaluate' human impacts, cover more than one impact and try to compare their severity — this is what gets you into the top mark band.
Learn one specific small-scale UK ecosystem (e.g. deciduous woodland) in detail — named species at each trophic level score more marks than vague answers.
Biodiversity answers should always explain WHY it matters (resilience, medicines, food, climate regulation) not just say 'it's important'.
revisionCardBullets: Ecosystem — community of organisms interacting with their non-living environment
Abiotic — non-living components (climate, soil, water, light)
Energy flow: Sun → Producer → Consumer → Decomposer (one direction only)
Nutrient cycling — decomposers return nutrients to soil; continuous loop
Interdependence — all species in an ecosystem rely on each other
Biome — large-scale ecosystem; distribution controlled by latitude and climate
Tropical rainforest at equator; hot desert at 30°N/S; tundra near poles
Deforestation, urbanisation, pollution, overgrazing, climate change all disrupt ecosystems
Biodiversity — variety of species; makes ecosystems more resilient
Knock-on effect — removing one species causes ripple effects up and down food web
Loss of biodiversity can lead to ecosystem collapse with major consequences for humans
